titleStmt.title: Letter written by Eileen Doris Meyer to family members during Easter Week 1916
Letters 1916-1923
titleStmt.author: Eileen Doris Meyer

editionStmt.p: This work was originally published by Maynooth University in Ireland in 2017. In 2026 this data, stored in a relational database was extracted and converted into this TEI/XML document.

availability.p: This is an open access work licensed under Creative Commons Attribution NonCommercial 4.0 International license (CC BY-NC 4.0).

note.p:
Eileen Doris Meyer (1896 - 1976 ) was returning to Dublin from Derry when she was caught up the events of the Easter Rising with her family. Her letters function as a diary, detailing her time living in Rathmines avoiding the conflict. Meyer's account describes the excitement, confusion and weariness of living in a city in conflict.
Meyer's brother, Alan Meyer (1899 - 1918), who is mentioned throughout the letter, became a 2nd Lieutenant with the Royal Garrison Artillery some time after the letter was written. Meyer was killed in action 1918.

projectDesc.p: The data in these XML files was generated based on a data dump from the Magellan database (https://github.com/Maynooth-Center-for-Digital-Humanities/Magellan). Each database record has been converted to a JSON file (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/json). The element section of the JSON file contains the TEI-encoded contents of the letters. The TEI XML has been cleaned and made well-formed using a Python script (https://github.com/letters1916static/letters-data/tree/main/src).

body.ab:
Monday Left Derry at 3.30 and arrived at Porta-
down at 5.30. There was no mail train it being
a bank holiday so we had to wait till 6.30 for
a train. While we Miss Austin & I were having tea in the refresh-
ment room a chap called Pollock, a man
I had known in the Bank of Ireland came
to us & said that there was some talk that
we couldn't get through to Dublin as a
bridge had been blown up at Donabate.
This sounded quite exciting & I went to send
a telegram to inform them at home of the
fact, but was told that they couldn't get
through to Dublin at present; in the end
they took it & said they might get it through
by about 10 in the evening. When the train
came in it was very crowded & we all got
into a first class carriage. We were up to
time as far as Drogheda, and the Guard
went along the platform shouting 'first
stop Dublin' so we thought it must all
have been a mistake, but we stopped
next at Skerries for about half an
hour & then went on stopping so long
& so often that it was half ten by the
time we got to Amiens St. Then the ex-
citement began. Miss Austin had a lot
of luggage & there wasn't a cab or
a car or a taxi in the station. Pollock went to
telephone for a taxi, telephone wasn't
working & a porter kindly informed him
that no taxi man would risk his life
to come out. There were an awful lot
of soldiers about & they told us that they
had come up from the Curragh & were
guarding the station, that many other
stations & the post office were in the
hands of Sinn Feiners. They said
there had been two engagements in the streets, that the 5th Lancers & 10th Dublins had been engaged in. All this was very thrilling if you could have stayed where you were but as we had to get
right through the city it was a bit too thrilling.
Anyway we put the luggage in the left luggage
office which was packed & I heard a porter tell
a man that the waiting room was open but
was already full. Miss Austin thought we
ought to have an escort of soldiers but they
assured us we would be much worse off
with soldiers as they shot them but wouldn't
shoot us if we didn't annoy them! Well
we started off to walk as there were no
trams & barring the fact that we had to
go by the Quays because Earl St was barr-
acaded at the top. Earl St itself was all
broken glass etc., the town was absolutely
quiet & we arrived home without a single
solitary adventure - rather to our disapp-
ointment as we had been expecting so
much. But the family nearly fainted
with surprise when they saw us (they had
all gone to bed by the time I arrived)
Alan had gone down to Amiens St to wait
the 7 & had to dodge bullets all the way
down & when he got there was told there
was no mail, he waited till 9 & then the
military came & took possession & turned
him out saying there would be no more
trains that night. He was away from 6
till 10 & mother says his hair is greyer
since & Aunty says if hers hadn't been
white already it would be after that. They
were amazed that we had got home as
easily. We heard afterwards that a man
&his wife & child had gone through Stephens
green (the sinn Feiners, had possession of it) half
an hour before us && had all been shot, not
killed but they each of them had a bullet in
them. They started about 12 on Monday by
taking possession of the post office — the g.p.o.
the Castle, the City Hall, all railway stations
barring Amiens St. the magazine in the park
Stephens green && Jacobs. also Hatch St
which they baracadeed at one end with
gilbeys barrels && th other with motor cars
from the garage at the end. Some of
them got into Davy's the public house at
Portobello Bridge & they shot every soldier
who crossed the Bridge. Only for Noel Scott
Alan would probably have been shot too.
Noel was home on leave on Sunday & as he
was going out for a walk with him alan
changed into muffle so when he was going
into hospital on Sunday evening he still
had it on. When he was coming back on
Monday morning first about 12 they were
shooting the soldiers from Davys, he
saw one shot & only for Noel he would
have been in uniform himself. He
wanted to get into it then & go & report at
Portabello & v volunteer for "active service"
but Father would not let him & mother
locked up his uniform.
4
Thursday. This morning everyone rallied forth to
barriers wondering whether the military had
dished the S.F altogether or not. There was a
fearful crowd in Rathmines everyone was in
groups talking when alan && I got as far as
Portobello Bridge we met Tom Lawlor who
ass implored us not to go any further as when
he got as far as the green the bullets were
flying & he had to run for his life (they had
got them out of Davys, Harcourt St. station &
Hatch St, by this time) we said we'd go on
at any rate &all the bullets so we went on.
The Rathmines section of the Bank of Ireland
male & female was grouped in Harcourt
St & everyone else was standing about
talking. Joe Walker was on his door step
conversing with a large crowd & warned
us not to go further than the Ivanhoe
Hotel. I met some of the men from the
office coming back. They said there were
shots in the green & they were not going to
risk their skins when not risking it one as
a holiday we went as far as the corner of the
green where there was a large crowd standing
some people who were fond of work were
going on, of course the shots were really only
up in the air they were not trying to shoot
civilians, if you were shot it was an
accident unless you were a soldier, but
a good many accidents happened. The
people who did go on (it was no use going
down Camden St for they potted at you
there from Jacobs) & arrive at the offices
& banks were sent home again as there
was no business being done. I came
and took about an hour to get home,as you
met so many people & had to stop and discuss
the situation with them all. Then I came out
again with Father, and anything like the
crowd in Rathmines standing around doing
nothing! We went on to Gainforts then, Lil
& Nesa had gone down to Marabrough
the day before & hadn't arrived home. Uncle
Willie had managed to get down town
in the morning, & there wasn't even a counter
left whole in Leivers much less any of
the stock & even the books were gone.
Nearly all the shops in Sackville St. had
been looted the same way. In the afternoon it poured & no one went
out except Alan who went right down
town into the thick of it, just to see the
fun. In the evening it cleared up
& Rathmines was quite gay, all the
shops were open & the Picture Houses
& everyone was about, you'd never
think there was war in the city. Wednesday. People started off for
business again & the same thing happened.
Most of them turned back again &
stood round Rathmines discussing
the situation. Martial law was
declared & all the shops were shut, you
had to get in the side doors to buy
anything. Mother got tired standing
so long so Father & I started off for
Findlaters & bought up all the tea &
sugar etc we could get, & carried them
home, imagine Father carrying home parcels of
groceries! We went round to Gainforts
again in the afternoon. Lil & Nesa had got
home that morning. They had got into King & #x2014;
bridge the evening before, and of course
could get no conveyance, and as they could
not possibly walk to Ranelagh, they went
for the night to Herbert Smiths, where they
went up to their bedroom & while trying to
light the gas a bullit came through
the window. Lil declares she couldn't
see for half an hour she got such a
shock. Things were very bad round there
that night & it seems that they flicked
the light about so much the military
thought they were signalling to the
Sinn Feiners & fired, anyway they had
a very narrow escape. The next morn #x2014;
ing they had to walk all the way home,
& had the greatest difficulty to pass
some of the sentries. We had some
difficulty in getting back from Gain —
sfort ourselves as they were searching
Oakley Rd, fortunately there was a nice
Dublin Fusilier officer at the end of the
road & he let us pass. You couldn't get
further up Rathmines Rd than Richmond
Hill either. The only house we could
visit with any certainty of getting back
was the Cronhelms - & and Dorothy had
measles. It really is very thrilling to
go out & not know whether you can
get back or not. Of course Mother &
Aunty are in a fit every morning Alan goes
down town, as the bullits are flying there.
7 Thursday. No one attempted to go into business
today but went chasing food instead. The
queues outside Findlaters & Williams were
tremendous. Magees were Sinn Feiners &
had all departed on Monday. Mother got
rabbits in McCabes, they'd absolutely no fish,
& got a steak & also a joint for Sunday in
Keoghs, he charged mother 1/6 lb but own
customers 2/- , & if he gave them any at all. We
were in despair about groceries till Annie
Starkie told us Cooks were open if you
knocked at the side door. I had to go back
to Rathmines so Mother went round by
Charleville Rd. with a 1/4 stone packet of
oat meal under each arm & Cooks
little boy beside her with a basket full
of the rest of the things. There were
sentries all over Rathmines & just at
the corner of Wynnfield Rd, where they were
particularly guarding the Post office &
the telephone, if you passed with your
hands in your pockets you heard a
peremptory "take your hands out of
your pockets". He wasn't a very good
tempered sentry either & was getting
annoyed at everyone's broad grin as
they displayed both hands. There was a proclamation posted all
over the place this morning announcing
that everyone was to be in their houses
between the hours of 7.30 pm. & 5.30. a.m.
Imagine that! and actually by twenty —
five to eight there wasn't a person up
or down the road. I'd never have thought
Dublin people would be so obedient to
8 a proclamation. I forgot to mention we
have had no gas since Monday so its pretty
miserable with candles, and today a
soldier went all along our side of the
road & told us to pull down our blinds
in the back & leave them down all day &
at night to shut the shutters or at any
rate not to show any light. The
blinds down in the day time is so that
we won't see the prisoners being shot
in the barracks. When we were at supper in the break—
fast room (of course there's firing near
& far going on all the time) but all of
a sudden a bullit was fired down
the lane under the arch. I can tell
you we did jump, it sounded as if it
was right in the room. I don't wonder
Lil couldn't see for half an hour after
the bullit came into their room. Edith
got such a fright she wouldn't sleep
downstairs so she brought up her
mattress & camped out on the landing.
After this when soldiers home on leave
or war book mentions the whiz of the
bullits flying past, the noise of the
machine gun or the dull noise roar of the
artillery in the distance we can say
quite wisely "oh" yes, we know all about
it" We can tell at once quite off handidly
now whether a shot is rifle (Service or Sinn
Feinn) machine gun, artillery or guns from
a ship. In fact we have all become
quite war worn veterans.
9 Friday.
This morning there was much the same
fight for food but we had all we wanted
except bread, our breadman never came &
you couldn't get bread for love or money
in Rathmines so mother had to turn
round & bake soda Bread. We have
plenty of flour but not much coal, its
well its such lovely weather we don't
need fires. The firing was simply terrific
all last night the worst there's been
yet. Some English soldiers came in on
Wednesday afternoon with a lot of
machine guns & artillery. Alan was
walking along with them & one of them
asked him to "spell the name of what
we're going to fight." I'm not saying
anything about the fighting in town as
I'm sure you'll see it all in the papers
& know more about it than we do ourselves
We've absolutely no outside
news as everyone says "the other war might
be over for all we know!" We really don't
know much about our own war either as
all we have to go by are eye witnesses
reports & as they everyone differ it's very
hard to know what's what. The Irish
Times came out in town on Tues, Wed, &
Thurs. but all it did was to comment on
our lack of news & give the people good
advice about going home at 7.30 etc. It
also said it was very good to see such
absolute lack of panic everywhere, and
to see people walking about the streets
10
with bullets flying all round them as if nothing
was happening, but all the same it would
like to point out that the military were not
giving a public entertainment, & people
would do a lot better to stay at home. We're beginning to have spasms in
Rathmines now, when the soldiers get it
into their heads to clear Rathmines Rd.
(I suppose they think they see Sinn F's
in the crowd) & they charge & yell. You
should see the people flying up Leinster
Rd. its great fun, if they loiter round the
end of the road to see whats going on
in Rathmines, the soldiers line across
& point their rifles at them & then they
melt into thin air. Grosvenor Rd was
searched yesterday evening & because
Mrs Briscoe went to open the dining room
window from the bottom a soldier pointed
his rifle at her, I'm sure she had six
fits but history doesn't record. There have been so many alarms &
excursions & Rathmines today & the shoot -
ing sounds so very near ( the S.Fs are all <lb/ along the canal & S.C.R. shooting at the
Barracks) that nobody likes to venture
far from home, and as its a glorious
day everyone's sitting on their front
steps. Never before have people been
so friendly with their neighbours, but
they're about the only people you can
visit with any safety now. Alan's
leave was up to-day so he insisted on going to
Portobello but he forgot to go until nearly seven.
The adjutant wasn't there so he was told to come
again in the morning. He saw two officers, one
told him to keep out of it if could, and
the other asked him between his intervals
of cursing the candle (imagine nothing but
candles in Portobello) why he hadn't come
sooner if he wanted to see some fun. He
thinks the first officer was English & the
second Irish. They were all ducking &
dodging about in the barracks to avoid
the bullets coming over the wall from
the canal. He had to get a pass from the officer then. for being out after 7.30 I forgot to say that this morning Auntie
had one of her old attacks. Mother could
get nothing but corn beef for dinner yester -
day so it must have been that, but she
isn't very bad. Saturday
No bread to be had for love or
money, hundreds deep round every bakery
door. Sam & Ethel were up & they had no
bread either. In about an hour Sam
came back with loaves under each arm
if it had been bags of gold we couldn't
have been more delighted or astonished.
There had been an awful crowd round
Wiltons when the cart arrived & you know
they keep the iron gate thing shut &
serve the people over it, and the breadman
lifts in the trays of bread over the people's
heads to the girls in the shop. Sam was
near the front & he said to the girls, if
you let me in I'll lift those trays for
you, so they let him in & he was
serving bread behind the counter for about
half an hour or until it was all finished.
of course he kept enough for himself & us
we've enough now to do us over Sunday.
There was another spasm in Rathmines
today & Mother if you please got stuck
between two sentries in Wynnfield Rd
& neither would let her past, she was
running from one to the other till at
last one of them shouted to the other
"let her past for the time being". Alan & I started off for Selskar Terr. &
when we reached Rathmines found it
had been cleared, Alan insisted on
going on till we were stopped but you
feel horribly conspicuous & a great
target for a bullet being the only
person in Rathmines, I was very glad
when we had safely turned into Castle —
wood Avenue. When we got to Ranelagh
we heard two women had been shot
just outside Johnson's five minutes
before. Lil & Nesa came back
with me as they wanted some books, of
course by that time Rathmines was
if anything more crowded than ever, it
always is half an hour after it has
been cleared. We were standing on
our steps when we saw a crowd of
soldiers coming up the road & more in
Rathmines going on to Portobello.
Just as the advance guard got as far
as our house they stopped for a rest
13 & we went out to talk to them, they looked
awfully hot as they'd marched from
Kingstown, of course they were English
chaps & had terrific English accents.
I went in & got them all the oranges
there were in the house. They said they
thought this was a great country every
one had been giving them things all
the way from Kingstown till they
could hardly carry any more. After they had gone we heard it was
all over, everyone was informing everyone
else that it was all over but we
hardly believed it as we still heard
shooting. As a matter of fact it was
all over in the city but they continued
firing in the suburbs for some time. I forgot to say that we actually got
some English papers this morning.
There was a fearful rush for them & you
had to pay 2d for 1/2 d papers. Some shop
in Ranelagh got hold of just about
six London "Times" & charged 1/- each for
them & got it too. Sunday Ethel has got measles, cer -
tainly things always come in a lump,
of course Baby is bound to get them,
as long as Sam doesn't it will be
all right. We had church same as usual
this morning, & had a lot of the green
people as of course they had no church.
Alan went down to the adelaide to see
if he could get some of his belongings
(I forgot to mention that they turned him
out on last Tuesday they were so full up)
He forgot to bring back any of his things
so he had one pair of pyjamas there &
had sent to Southwold for his other
pair also socks etc & expects that they
were in the G.P.O. so he'll not see
them again. We had church at 6 in the evening
so as to be home by 7.30 but just
as Fr. Alley was beginning his sermon
just getting warmed up you know at
the beginning to fling his arms about,
the military sent a polite request that
everyone would go home so everyone
departed. The shooting near us has
never been so bad as this evening.
Father & I were standing at the gate
talking to Mr. Cordnen who was hold —
ing forth on the ridiculous nervousness
of his wife & daughter jumping at every
shot as if it was meant for them, if
people would only have some common-
sense! etc! etc! etc! etc! when whiz bang
a bullet landed in the hawthorn tree
beside us & you shouldhave seen Mr
Cordnen skip, he said good bye & was
half way to the Square before we'd realised
he had gone.
we have lots of flour but no coal scarcely. 15 Monday No bread again. Alan & I had to
walk over to Ballsbridge (Johnson & Mooneys)
for some. and I can tell you its some
walk there & back without a rest & a
couple of loaves to carry back & a very
hot day. But the joke of it was everyone
thought they go early to avoid the
crush & some started off at six in the
morning & had to wait in a queue for
about an hour & then only got two
loaves, we started at eleven, hadn't
to wait at all & got four loaves.
But you should have seen the stream
of people you might almost say a
procession that we met coming
back when we were going, everyone
had loaves tucked under their arm
of course without any paper, some
people bought pillow cases in which
they put the bread & then slung it
over their shoulders. Of course for
people to carry bread without paper
is nothing now. You've seen people
this week, who before wouldn't have
carried a nice neat parcel out of
town, with a cabbage tucked under
one arm minus paper of course, &
a slice of meat in the other hand
and equally minus paper. Really &
truly that wasn't a bit an unusual
sight. Nothing much happened
16 today & no shots were heard at all until
about 6.30 when they were fast & furious
till dark. They say there are snipers on
the roofs of these houses. You've no idea how funny it is about
twenty five to eight to see people scurrying
home for all their worth, who before
wouldn't have thought of scurrying at
twenty five to twelve. Then by about
half eight or as soon as it has got dark
you feel as if you were up in the middle
of the night nobody passes up or down
& the only thing you hear are rifle shots
& if you look out all the houses are dark
as the people in the opposite side of the
road all migrate to the back of the
house. If it only hadn't been for
this half seven dodge we'd have had
a grand time & so exciting but the
evenings are fearfully boring & quite
counteract the excitement of the day —
time. Everyone goes to bed at nine
so there aught to be plenty of beauty
in Dublin now everyone has had
so much beauty sleep.
17 Tuesday about 8 I woke up & heard soldiers
marching up the street I got up to watch them
& they went into a house beside the lane leading
to the Barracks. I thought if they were going
to search all the houses I'd better get dressed,
so I did. They got two prisoners in that
house (No 31) & marched them off, the officer
had a lot of papers under his arm. They
searched a lot of the houses round here this
morning, there was so much sniping last
night but they didn't search us. I was
quite disappointed it would have been fun. The English soldiers here are quite en —
joying themselves, someone heard a sentry
in Rathmines remark that in London
you never got were never given a cigarette but
since he came over here he got so
many he wished he could smoke ten
at once & as for drinks he could get
full up at that public house as often
as he wanted, he thought it was a grand
country but he didn't see what they
wanted to be fighting for. But the English
are always so dense that it's utterly im -
possible to make them understand
the why or the wherefore of the rebellion or
who or what the Sinn Feiners are.
Another soldier said to Alan that he
couldn't understand this country, one
day they gave you cigarettes & drinks
&anything you wanted & next day
they shot you. You can go into town now if you
18 like to see the sights' but there are sentries at
all the bridges & even have to have a pass
except men (note the men!) under 14 &
over 65 so Father just says 65 & walks past
the sentries. The whole thing has done
Father heaps of good, I suppose with bullets
flying round & artillery thundering you forget
you have bronchitis & a heart. Anyway he
walked down to Sackville St (no trams yet)
walked round there & walked back again,
and it hardly took anything out of him. I went down town this afternoon
with Lil & Nesa & its simply awful, Sackville
St. is a wreck, but I suppose you see
pictures of it in the paper. The office is the
first house standing in Eden Quay but
its quite all right has only one bullet
through it. However the rebellions over now so
so will this letter be. We'll have quite
an exciting time now finding out about
everyone 'cause of course we know nothing
about anyone. Mollie Haggard went to
Kerry for Easter & I don't know if she got
back, we don't know whether Hilda & Babs got
back from Newcastle & I don't know if
anything happened Robert Northridge or
Grovie Morgan as of course they were fight —
ing, in fact all the world except Rathmines
might be dead for all we know.
Mrs Meyer,
Gleaneen
Rostrevor
Co. Down
